---
title: "statsquiz27"
output:
  pdf_document: default
  html_document: default
date: "2025-02-07"
author: Katrina Churchill
---

```{r setup, include=FALSE}
knitr::opts_chunk$set(echo = TRUE)
```

## R Markdown

This is an R Markdown document. Markdown is a simple formatting syntax for authoring HTML, PDF, and MS Word documents. For more details on using R Markdown see <http://rmarkdown.rstudio.com>.

When you click the **Knit** button a document will be generated that includes both content as well as the output of any embedded R code chunks within the document. You can embed an R code chunk like this:

```{r Problem 1}
#Problem 1
library(dplyr)
data(storms)

#Part a
ncol(storms)

#Part b
nrow(storms)

#Part c
??storms
#wind in knots & pressure in millibars
```

## Problem 2

```{r Problem 2}
#part a
hist(storms$wind)

#Partb
max_wind_storm <- storms |>
  filter(wind == max(wind)) |>
  select(year, wind)

print(max_wind_storm)
```

## Problem 3

```{r}
#Problem 3
katrina_data <- storms |>
  filter(name == "Katrina" & year == "2005") 

plot(katrina_data$long, katrina_data$lat)
```

## Problem 4

```{r}
#problem 4
#Part a
clean <- c(53, 58)
swear <- c(69,78, 87, 140)

mean(clean)
mean(swear)
mean(swear) - mean(clean)

#Part b
t.test(clean, swear)
#pvalue = 0.095
```

## Problem 5

```{r}
#Problem 5
#part a

combined <- c(clean, swear)
replicated <- replicate(10000,{
  resample <- sample(combined) 
  mean(resample[3:6])-mean(resample[1:2])
})
  
table(abs(replicated)>=38)


#part b
hist(combined, breaks =20)

```
